item.title: Global markets recoil as Rubio warns war in Iran could stretch for weeks
item.description: The increase in oil prices and potential escalation of the conflict in Iran have led to decreased risk appetite.

item.title: Nato allies to train with Ukraine as Europe seeks closer defence ties
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Europe races to fund fusion

item.title: What the 1970s can teach investors about today’s oil shock
item.description: As central bankers tread the line between controlling inflation and avoiding economic stagnation, investors face tough decisions

item.title: Fed could cut balance sheet by $2tn without market turmoil, says Miran
item.description: Bank governor says regulatory changes and reduced stigma around lending tools could allow a gradual reduction in assets

item.title: Inflation and interest rates tracker: see how your country compares
item.description: Inflationary pressures are beginning to wane. Explore the latest data through interactive charts

item.title: The realities of economic security
item.description: The Peterson Institute for International Economics presents a high-level analytical discussion of the realities of economic security, examining real-world trade-offs and vulnerabilities in today's geopolitically fractured world. Anjali Bhatt, host of PIIE Insider Live, moderates a candid discussion with PIIE experts: Warwick McKibbin, who presents global macroeconomic scenarios of the impact of the Middle East conflict; Cullen Hendrix, who discusses vulnerabilities of the global economy to the disrupted supply of commodities including food, fuel, and industrial inputs; and Jacob Funk…

item.title: Half-baked lessons from 2022 yet to help Sri Lanka amid Iran oil crisis
item.description: Half-baked lessons from 2022 yet to help Sri Lanka amid Iran oil crisis

item.title: Energy crisis hits currencies of Asian economies reliant on Middle East
item.description: Energy crisis hits currencies of Asian economies reliant on Middle East

item.title: The Pentagon Wants Dual-Use Innovation. Patent Law Might Punish It.
item.description: In 1918, the U.S. Supreme Court held that government contractors could be sued for patent infringement even if their products were manufactured specifically for the U.S. government. Franklin D. Roosevelt, then acting secretary of the Navy, wrote an urgent letter to Congress warning that contractors were hesitant to build equipment for the Navy that could expose them to expensive litigation. Congress responded swiftly, amending federal law to shield contractors from patent suits when they were manufacturing products for the government. That statutory immunity, now codified as 28 U.S.C. § 1498, has protected defense manufacturers for over a century. But a The post The Pentagon Wants Dual-Use Innovation. Patent Law Might Punish It. appeared first on War on the Rocks .

item.title: Nuclear restart moves forward in Taiwan amid energy supply concerns
item.description: Nuclear restart moves forward in Taiwan amid energy supply concerns

item.title: China chip sector targets 80% self-sufficiency with US in its sights

item.title: China's COSCO ships appear to abort Strait of Hormuz transit
item.description: China's COSCO ships appear to abort Strait of Hormuz transit

item.title: The Iran Conflict Is Good News for Chinese Automakers
item.description: The increase in oil prices may accelerate the transition to electric vehicles, benefiting Chinese automakers.

item.title: Russia Warns of Force Majeure on Oil Cargoes After Port Disruptions
item.description: Russia’s oil exporters are warning buyers that cargoes from its key Baltic ports may not be delivered at all. Russian producers are now saying they may declare force majeure on cargoes from key Baltic Sea ports after a sustained wave of Ukrainian drone strikes knocked out critical infrastructure this week. The epicenter is Ust-Luga, one of Russia’s most important export terminals, where oil loadings have been halted since Wednesday following repeated strikes from Ukraine and a fire that, as of Friday, was still burning. Industry sources…

item.title: $100 Oil Is Solving Russia's Budget Problem
item.description: Russia is getting an unexpected windfall from the war in the Middle East. The Kremlin’s oil revenues this month hit a four-year high as oil prices jumped to $100 per barrel amid the Iran war and the de facto closed Strait of Hormuz. Moscow expects so much additional revenues from the oil price spike that authorities are unlikely to downgrade Russia’s economic prospects, hold off on planned budget cuts, and even boost military spending on the war in Ukraine, Bloomberg reports, citing sources with knowledge of the matter. A month ago,…

item.title: Gulf States Weigh a Difficult Choice as Iran War Escalates
item.description: Caught in the crossfire of the United States and Israel's war with Iran, America's allies in the Persian Gulf face a stark choice: maintain their defensive posture or join the fight against the Islamic Republic. Iran has fired thousands of drones and missiles at US military and diplomatic facilities and damaged key energy infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates (UAE), Qatar, Bahrain, and Kuwait since the war began on February 28. By choking off the Strait of Hormuz, a major artery for global oil and gas supplies, Tehran has also…

item.title: A Key Russia-Iran Supply Line Remains Open Despite the War
item.description: All the bombs dropped by the United States and Israel over the past four weeks on Iran have not disrupted a key trade route with Russia that both Tehran and Moscow rely on to help keep their respective war efforts going. And Azerbaijan, despite its strong ties to both the United States and Israel, has played a crucial role in keeping the route open. Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexey Overchuk, in comments published by the Interfax news agency on March 25, said the “western branch” of the North-South trade corridor continued to operate…

item.title: Bitcoin traders see 53% odds of sub-$66K BTC by April 24
item.description: Bitcoin price slumped on Friday as uncertainty over the US economy and war in Iran negatively impacted stock and crypto markets.

item.title: Bitcoin dips under $66K as oil sparks 'unsustainable' US inflation risk
item.description: Bitcoin joined a risk-asset rout as oil-supply nerves sparked major US inflation warnings, with $70,000 in place as new BTC price resistance.

item.title: US lawmakers publish crypto tax proposal without Bitcoin tax exemption
item.description: The bill proposes exempting dollar-pegged stablecoins from gains or losses if the tokens remain tightly pegged to the underlying fiat currency.

item.title: Bitcoin ETFs log biggest outflows in 3 weeks as Iran war fears rise
item.description: US Bitcoin ETFs saw outflows of $171 million on Thursday as market participants feared another weekend escalation in the US-Israel conflict with Iran.

item.title: Whales, sharks buy 61,000 BTC in a month amid global uncertainty
item.description: Not all whales have been accumulating; two moved tens of millions of dollars to exchanges on March 19 as Bitcoin fell amid an escalation of the Iran conflict.
